title: Adjustable Glasses: How They Work and Who They're For
description: Adjustable glasses let you change the nose bridge, temple length, or lens angle for a custom fit. See how they work, top brands, and whether they're worth it.
tldr: Adjustable glasses feature flexible components — nose pads, temple tips, or bridge width — that you can modify at home for a better fit. They're ideal for people between standard frame sizes, those with asymmetric features, or anyone tired of glasses sliding or pinching. Most cost $150-$400 and work with any prescription lens type.

definition.text: Eyeglasses designed with user-modifiable components that allow the wearer to customize fit without professional tools. Common adjustable features include sliding nose pads, telescoping temples, flexible bridge width, and tiltable lens angles.

sections.content:
Standard glasses come in fixed sizes. If they don't fit perfectly, you visit an optician for adjustments. Adjustable glasses take a different approach — they build flexibility directly into the frame.

The most common adjustable features:

  • Adjustable nose pads: Silicone or rubber pads on arms that can be moved up, down, or angled to change how the frame sits on your nose bridge. This is actually the most common "adjustable" feature — most metal frames already have this.
  • Telescoping temples: Arms that slide in and out to change length. Useful if standard temple lengths (140mm, 145mm) don't fit your head size.
  • Flexible bridge: A bridge mechanism that widens or narrows to accommodate different nose widths.
  • Tiltable front: Some frames let you adjust the pantoscopic tilt (the angle of the lenses relative to your face), which affects how well you see through progressive lenses.

sections.h2: Who Benefits Most from Adjustable Glasses?
sections.content:
Adjustable frames solve specific fitting problems. They're particularly useful for:

  • People between sizes: If 140mm temples are too short but 150mm feels loose, adjustable temples hit that sweet spot.
  • Low or high nose bridges: Standard frames are designed for average nose bridge dimensions. If yours is flatter or more prominent, adjustable nose pads make a huge difference in comfort and positioning.
  • Asymmetric faces: Most faces aren't perfectly symmetrical. One ear might sit slightly higher than the other, or one side of the nose bridge might be different. Adjustable components let you compensate.
  • Kids and teens: Growing faces change dimensions regularly. Adjustable frames can extend their useful life by several months.
  • Online buyers: If you're ordering glasses without trying them on, adjustable features give you a safety net for fit issues.

That said, a well-fitted standard frame from an experienced optician often feels just as good. The professional fitting at an optical store accounts for all these variables from the start.

The adjustable glasses market breaks into several categories:

  • Standard frames with adjustable nose pads: The most common type. Most metal and many acetate frames from brands like Ray-Ban and Oakley include adjustable nose pads. These can be tweaked by you or your optician.
  • Sport/performance frames: Oakley, Nike, and similar brands often build in adjustable nose pieces and temple grips for active use. Designed to stay put during movement.
  • Adjustable-focus glasses: A separate category entirely. These use a dial or slider to change lens power, intended as emergency or travel glasses. They do not replace a proper prescription.
  • Universal-fit frames: Some newer brands design frames specifically for faces that standard sizing doesn't serve well — wider bridges, different temple curves, etc.
TypeWhat AdjustsPrice RangeBest For
Metal with nose padsNose pad angle/height$100 — $400Most people
Sport framesNose piece, temple grip$150 — $350Active lifestyles
Telescoping templeTemple length$200 — $400Between standard sizes

Here's the honest comparison: adjustable glasses give you control over fit, but a professional fitting gives you precision.

An optician adjusts frames using specialized tools — pliers, frame heaters, alignment jigs. They can bend metal temples to follow the exact curve behind your ear, heat acetate frames to reshape them, and verify that lenses are centered to your pupils. These adjustments are more precise than what any consumer-adjustable feature can achieve.

Adjustable features are best thought of as fine-tuning on top of a professional fit, not a replacement for one. Get your glasses fitted properly at your local optical store, and the adjustable components become useful for day-to-day tweaks — loosening temples when you have a headache, raising nose pads when wearing sunscreen.

Most optical stores including Charm Optical in Edmonton offer free ongoing adjustments for the life of your glasses. So even with non-adjustable frames, you're never stuck with a bad fit.

sections.h2: How to Adjust Your Glasses at Home
sections.content:
For glasses with adjustable features, here are safe at-home tweaks:

  • Nose pads: Gently press inward to tighten, outward to loosen. Angle them to match the sides of your nose. Equal pressure on both sides prevents crooked sitting.
  • Temple tips: If your temples have silicone grips, you can slide them forward or back for comfort. Don't force them past their intended range.
  • Telescoping temples: Extend or retract evenly on both sides. Uneven temples cause the frame to sit crooked.

What to not try at home:

  • Bending metal temples — you risk snapping them
  • Heating acetate frames with a hair dryer — uneven heat warps them permanently
  • Adjusting the lens tilt — this changes your optical axis and can cause headaches

For anything beyond basic nose pad and temple adjustments, bring your glasses to your optician. Most stores do this free of charge and it takes just a few minutes. Check with your local optical store about their adjustment policy.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter — no upfront costs and no claim forms to submit afterwards.

Whether you're buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the entire process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

A well-fitting frame does more than look good — it ensures your lenses perform correctly. Frames that sit too high or low shift the optical centres away from your pupils, causing blurry vision and eye strain. Frames that are too wide slide down your nose, while tight frames create pressure headaches behind the ears.

Three measurements define frame fit: lens width (the horizontal size of each lens opening), bridge width (the gap between the two lenses), and temple length (the arm that hooks behind your ear). These are printed on the inside of the temple arm as three numbers like 52-18-140. Your optician uses these alongside your face measurements to recommend frames that balance aesthetics and function.

This is why in-person fitting matters, especially for progressive lenses where vertical lens alignment is critical. At Charm Optical in Edmonton, the opticians take precise measurements using digital tools to ensure your frames and lenses work together perfectly. With over 500 frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, you'll find the right combination of style and fit. Most styles are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical with single vision lenses.

faqs.a: Adjustable glasses are worth it if you have specific fitting challenges — unusual nose bridge width, between standard sizes, or asymmetric features. For most people, a well-fitted standard frame from an optician works just as well. The adjustable features are a bonus, not a necessity.

Most glasses can be adjusted by an optician using professional tools. Metal frames are the most adjustable. Acetate frames can be reshaped with heat. Titanium is flexible but springs back to its original shape. Consumer-adjustable features (nose pads, telescoping temples) are specific to certain frame designs.

faqs.q: How much do adjustable glasses cost?
faqs.a: Adjustable glasses range from $100-$400 for the frame, depending on brand and features. The adjustable mechanism doesn't significantly increase cost — most mid-range metal frames already include adjustable nose pads. Total cost with lenses runs $200-$600.

Adjustable-focus glasses (with a dial to change lens power) work in an emergency or for temporary use, but they cannot replace proper prescription glasses. They don't correct astigmatism, can't provide different corrections for each eye, and the optical quality is significantly lower than prescription lenses.

faqs.q: Where can I get glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames. They offer comprehensive eye exams, same-day glasses, professional fitting, and direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers. Book online at see.charmoptical.ca.
